title: Contact lens discomfort

source.title: TFOS DEWS II Diagnostic Methodology report
source.population: People undergoing dry-eye symptom screening, diagnosis, subtype assessment, differential diagnosis, or monitoring.
source.setting: Ocular-surface symptom assessment and eye-care diagnostic evaluation.
source.exclusions: Exact for selected dry-eye symptom, diagnostic-test, ocular-surface, eyelid, and contact-lens context. It does not support primary-care autonomous diagnosis, treatment, medicine, referral urgency, fixed follow-up, or UAE service pathways.

exact_location.heading: Differential diagnosis, visual asthenopia, and contact lenses

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports screen-related visual discomfort, pain or photophobia as differential context, and contact-lens-associated dry-eye context without treatment instructions.

exact_location.heading: Differential diagnosis, visual asthenopia, and contact lenses

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports screen-related visual discomfort, pain or photophobia as differential context, and contact-lens-associated dry-eye context without treatment instructions.

assessment.text: Supports contact-lens use, usually unilateral redness, watering, foreign-body sensation, discomfort, photophobia, peripheral corneal opacity, and the need to distinguish sight-threatening microbial keratitis.
assessment.rationale: Supports contact-lens-associated keratitis presentation and differential safety for contact-lens discomfort; no decision is generated.

source.title: Clinical Management Guideline: Keratitis, contact-lens-associated infiltrative
source.population: Contact-lens wearers with suspected contact-lens-associated infiltrative keratitis.
source.exclusions: Exact for contact-lens-associated infiltrative keratitis features and safe lens-care documentation; UAE escalation pathways require clinician review.

exact_location.heading: Clinical summary — presentation and differential safety

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports contact-lens use, usually unilateral redness, watering, foreign-body sensation, discomfort, photophobia, peripheral corneal opacity, and the need to distinguish sight-threatening microbial keratitis.
